title: India · Value & Quality Picks · May 2026
analystVoice: Senior value analyst · Khandelwal lens · pay-up-for-quality-but-not-for-hype
methodologyNote: Five-phase funnel — universe → quantitative quality gates → valuation gates → qualitative moat check → macro overlay. Target: 8–12 names diversified across 6+ sectors with at least one clear value angle each.

valuation: Available at a meaningful margin to peak multiple

macroNotes: India broad market is fairly-to-fully valued after 2020–2024 run; mid/smallcap froth corrected in 2025 but still selective.
FII flows turned net positive in early 2026 after 2025 outflow cycle.
Best risk/reward is in (a) large-cap quality that de-rated on transient concerns and (b) PSU monopolies with deep dividend yields.
Capital-goods cycle is at peak multiples; selective avoidance even where fundamentals are good.

exclusions.reason: Peak-cycle order-book multiples; consensus crowded trade; mean-reversion risk severe.

exclusions.theme: Capital goods at 60–80× (ABB India, Siemens India, CG Power)
exclusions.reason: Pricing-in flawless execution for a decade; no margin of safety.
No durable moat at current scale; not yet value.
Broker-driven; quality of earnings unproven; governance risk elevated.
Quality is real, price is not. Will be on watchlist if they de-rate 25%+.
Premium to large-cap IT for incremental growth that may not sustain.

picks.name: HDFC Bank Ltd.
picks.thesis: India's best banking franchise — best CASA, lowest credit costs, deepest distribution.
picks.valueAngle: Trading at ~2.5× P/B vs historical 3.5×. Post-HDFC-Ltd merger digestion is largely done; ROA normalising back to 1.9%+ over next 4–6 quarters. Re-rating play as the merger overhang clears and unsecured-retail concerns moderate.

picks.name: State Bank of India
picks.thesis: India's largest bank by assets — cleaned-up balance sheet, ROA 1%+, ROE 16–18%.
picks.valueAngle: At ~1.4–1.6× P/B (subsidiaries-adjusted), classic PSU-bank value. Massive Tier-1 capital, India-growth proxy, dividend yield 1.7%+. Trades at a deep discount to private-bank peers despite credit-cost normalisation.

picks.name: Tata Consultancy Services
picks.thesis: India's IT cash-flow factory — $30B+ revenue, ~50% ROE, near-100% FCF conversion, generational client relationships.
picks.valueAngle: Trading at ~22× earnings vs historical 28–30× as the market frets about generative-AI cannibalisation. The franchise is mispriced: AI is a deflationary tailwind that TCS captures via velocity, not pricing — long lag, but the moat remains intact. Tata Sons holds 72% — alignment is real.

picks.name: ITC Ltd.
picks.thesis: Cigarettes are a structurally-shrinking cash machine that funds a scaling FMCG portfolio (Aashirvaad, Sunfeast, Bingo) and a recently-demerged hotels business. ROCE 35%+.
picks.valueAngle: Trades at ~25× earnings with 3.5% dividend yield. Tobacco cash flow underwrites the FMCG ramp; hotels demerger crystallised value. ESG-driven sell-down is the bear case, but the cash flow doesn't care.

picks.name: Asian Paints Ltd.
picks.thesis: India's #1 decorative paints franchise (~50% share) with the most-feared dealer network in consumer India. ROE 25%+ historically.
picks.valueAngle: De-rated ~35–40% from peak on Birla Opus entry fears. Pricing power and dealer lock-in are intact; competitive losses will be slower and smaller than market expects. Available at ~50× earnings vs historical 65–70× — quality at a 30% discount to its own history is the trade.

picks.name: Bajaj Auto Ltd.
picks.thesis: Two-wheeler and three-wheeler leader with the most geographically diversified franchise in Indian auto (50%+ exports to Africa, LATAM, ASEAN). Net cash ~₹15,000Cr; ROE 25%+.
picks.valueAngle: Available at ~22× earnings. EV transition is real — Chetak EV scaling profitably; new electric three-wheeler portfolio launching. Premium two-wheeler franchise (KTM, Triumph) under-appreciated. Dividend yield 3%+.

picks.name: Larsen & Toubro Ltd.
picks.thesis: India's engineering champion — ~₹5 lakh Cr order book; dominant in transmission, urban infra, defense, hydrocarbons, Middle East EPC.
picks.valueAngle: Core-EPC business available at ~22× earnings if you back out IT subsidiaries (LTIM, LTTS) and financial services. Middle East orderbook re-acceleration is under-appreciated. Defense indigenisation is a 10-year tailwind that just started.

picks.name: Sun Pharmaceutical Industries
picks.thesis: India's largest pharma franchise — domestic formulations leader; US specialty (Ilumya, Cequa, Winlevi) scaling; EM presence.
picks.valueAngle: Available at ~28× earnings as the market under-weights specialty earnings durability. India domestic franchise alone deserves a 25× multiple; specialty is the optionality. Net cash; ROE 17–18%.

picks.name: Coal India Ltd.
picks.thesis: Monopoly coal supplier (~80% of India's coal production) — government-owned, near-zero debt, ₹100,000Cr+ cash on books.
picks.valueAngle: Trades at ~7× earnings with dividend yield 7–8%. ESG-driven sell-down has gone too far: India will burn coal for another 20 years even as renewables scale. Cash is being deployed into critical minerals, renewables, and aluminium — the optionality is free at this multiple.

picks.name: Maruti Suzuki India Ltd.
picks.thesis: Passenger-vehicle market leader (~40% share); deepest distribution and service network in India; Suzuki parent technology backbone.
picks.valueAngle: At ~25× earnings, market is under-weighting (a) SUV portfolio refresh now matching market mix, (b) export ramp into Africa/MEA via Suzuki global platform, (c) net cash ₹50,000Cr+. Hybrid-first strategy vs pure-EV is a feature, not a bug, for India's charging-poor reality.

notesOnConvictionRanking: Ranks are heuristic — based on combination of (a) margin of safety, (b) quality durability, (c) catalyst visibility over 12–24 months. HDFC Bank #1 is the highest-conviction risk-adjusted; Coal India #9 is the deepest-value but most controversial. All 10 expected to be analysed using the value-analyst skill before any position is initiated.

reviewTriggers: Any pick falls > 30% (re-check thesis; potential add)
Sector-level regime change (e.g., RBI policy pivot, oil shock, INR move > 5%)
